summary: Joel presents a table showing the devastating stock price collapses of thirteen Silicon Alley (New York-based internet) companies, all of which have lost between 78% and 97% of their value from their 52-week highs. The post is a snapshot of the dot-com bubble bursting in October 2000, with companies like Juno, TheGlobe.com, and Priceline losing over 90% of their market value. Joel offers no commentary beyond the raw data, letting the numbers speak for themselves. He closes with a brief personal note apologizing for a delay in his Painless Specs series because he has a cold.
key_points: Thirteen prominent Silicon Alley companies had lost between 78% and 97% of their stock value from 52-week highs by October 2000
The data captures the dot-com bubble collapse in real time, with well-known names like Priceline, DoubleClick, and Razorfish devastated
Joel lets the raw numbers make the argument without any editorial commentary, making the carnage self-evident
money_quotes: Silicon Alley stocks aren't doing too well.
and I have a cold. Sorry for the delay in posting part 4 of Painless Specs.
key_insight: The dot-com bubble's collapse was so dramatic that a simple table of stock losses — most exceeding 90% — needed no commentary to convey the magnitude of the destruction.
